name: Eta H1-over-TLS handshake latency under concurrent load

description: Baseline: Eta H1-over-TLS handshake latency under concurrent load (oha --disable-keepalive, c=16, n=2000, median of 3). p50 2.69ms; handshake = ECDHE-RSA RSA-2048 sign serialized on single core.

asi.hypothesis: baseline of Eta HTTPS (H1-over-TLS) handshake latency under concurrent load (oha --disable-keepalive, c=16, fresh TLS handshake per request, median of 3)
asi.diagnosis: The broad-suite H1 TLS ~15-16ms p99 is entirely TLS handshake cost. Full handshake = ECDHE-RSA with RSA-2048 server signature (~1.4ms CPU); at c=16 they serialize on the single-core Eio server (p50 2.69ms). Steady-state H1 TLS request latency is already fine (~0.2ms). TCP_NODELAY already set; Tls_eio context built once per listener.
asi.baseline: h1_tls_hs_p50_us=2689us, hs_p99=8668us, hs_rps=4692, ka_p99=45269us (noisy n=1000 keep-alive symptom), peak_rss=51MB
asi.levers: 1) TLS session resumption/tickets (skip RSA sign) -- verify oha resumes. 2) Remove per-handshake with_timeout fork in run_https_connection. 3) Parallelize CPU-bound handshakes across domains (attacks c=16 serialization). 4) TLS1.3+X25519 fast path. 5) mirage-crypto RSA backend.
asi.next: Start with the cheapest structural win: remove the per-handshake with_timeout sleeper fork in run_https_connection (same anti-pattern that gave H1 plain -45%). Then investigate handshake parallelism across domains (the c=16 serialization is the biggest multiplier).
